planting.note: Plant divisions in spring in moist to flooded, brackish to fresh, slightly acidic to neutral soil in full sun; black needlerush is a dominant emergent rush of coastal salt and brackish marshes along the Gulf and Atlantic coasts from Texas to Virginia. It is critical for coastal marsh restoration projects.

directions: Plant divisions in moist to flooded, brackish to freshwater, slightly acidic to neutral soil in full sun along coastal marsh edges, estuarine shorelines, and salt-tolerant wet swales; black needlerush is the dominant emergent plant of the upper and middle portions of Gulf and Atlantic coastal salt and brackish marshes, forming dense, nearly impenetrable stands of sharp-tipped, dark green to black-tipped cylindrical stems. It is one of the most important plants for Gulf Coast salt marsh ecology — the dense stands provide nesting and foraging habitat for seaside sparrows, rails, and wading birds; shelter and food for fiddler crabs and marsh invertebrates; and significant shoreline stabilization through dense rhizome networks. Unlike smooth cordgrass (Spartina alterniflora), which dominates lower marsh elevations that are regularly flooded, black needlerush dominates the higher marsh zones flooded only on spring tides. Essential for coastal marsh restoration.

planting.note: Plant rhizome divisions in spring in shallow water (0–12 inches depth) or permanently moist soil in full sun; narrowleaf cattail grows naturally in shallow marshes, pond margins, and ditches and spreads aggressively by rhizomes to form dense stands. Better suited to moderate-sized water features than broadleaf cattail.

directions: Plant rhizome divisions in spring in shallow water (0–12 inches depth) or saturated soil in full sun; narrowleaf cattail is a cosmopolitan aquatic native with narrower leaves than broadleaf cattail (T. latifolia) and a distinctive gap between the male and female portions of the brown, sausage-like seed head — a reliable identification feature. Like all cattails, it provides extraordinary wildlife value: the fluffy seed cotton is used for nest lining by ducks and wrens; the seed heads provide food for marsh wrens and red-winged blackbirds; the dense rhizome mats provide spawning habitat for fish and shelter for amphibians. All parts of cattail are edible — the young spring shoots taste like cucumber; the green male flower heads can be cooked like corn on the cob; the pollen is a protein-rich flour substitute; and the rhizomes yield a starchy flour. Spreads rapidly — contain in pots sunk in water or with barriers to prevent unwanted spread.

planting.note: Direct sow in spring in well-drained, dry, rocky, lean, alkaline to neutral soil in full sun; rock muhly grows on dry, rocky canyon walls, limestone outcrops, and talus slopes of the Chihuahuan Desert and surrounding mountains. Extremely drought and heat tolerant.

directions: Plant in well-drained, dry, rocky, lean, alkaline to neutral soil in full sun; rock muhly is a small, delicate native grass of rocky canyon walls and limestone outcrops in the Chihuahuan Desert of Texas, New Mexico, and Mexico, adapted to the extreme drainage and heat of vertical rock faces where few other plants survive. The very fine, hair-like foliage forms small, wispy tufts that root into rock crevices, and the airy, purple-tinted seed heads in fall create a delicate haze of color against the rock. Extremely drought-tolerant once its roots reach the moisture retained in rock crevices. A specialist plant for dry, rocky, calcareous scree and rock garden settings in the arid Southwest. More drought-tolerant than any other commonly available muhly grass. Excellent for planting in crevices of dry limestone walls and rocky garden outcrops in zone 9.

planting.note: Plant divisions in spring in permanently moist to shallow-water conditions in full sun to partial shade; dwarf papyrus is a miniature African papyrus well-suited to container water gardens, small ponds, and indoor/outdoor aquatic settings. Remove offsets regularly to maintain vigor.

directions: Plant in permanently moist to shallow-water soil or grow in a pot sitting in water in full sun to partial shade; dwarf papyrus is a diminutive South African relative of the famous Nile papyrus (Cyperus papyrus), growing to only 18–30 inches — compact enough for container water gardens, bird baths, and small aquatic pots that full-sized papyrus would overwhelm. The characteristic papyrus umbel of thread-like rays topped with tiny flower clusters is present in miniature, giving the same architectural, firework-display appearance as giant papyrus on a much more manageable scale. In zone 9a, it can be grown outdoors year-round in a frost-protected microclimate or as a container plant moved indoors in winter. Excellent for small-space water gardens, tabletop ponds, and indoor aquatic gardens. Propagates readily by division and by floating the spent flower heads in water — the ray tips root to produce new plantlets.

planting.note: Plant rhizome divisions in spring in moist to shallow-water conditions in full sun; graceful cattail is a smaller, more refined Eurasian cattail well-suited to smaller water gardens and ponds where the native broadleaf or narrowleaf cattail would be too large and vigorous.

directions: Plant rhizome divisions in spring in moist to shallow-water conditions in full sun; graceful cattail is a Eurasian species intermediate in size between dwarf cattail (T. minima) and the common cattails, reaching only 3–5 feet — making it considerably more manageable for smaller water gardens. The brown seed heads are smaller and more graceful than those of common cattails, and the gap between male and female sections is more pronounced and consistent. While spreading by rhizomes as all cattails do, it is less aggressively spreading than broadleaf cattail. Provides excellent wildlife habitat at a human-garden scale: the seed cotton, young shoots, and pollen are all usable as described for other cattail species. A refined and underused alternative to native cattails for ornamental water gardens and small ponds where scale is important.
